description: Realistic local onboarding fixture for the MA-GA-Agent onboarding-memory slice. One CS/AI-focused MS applicant with resume, transcript, writing sample, structured answers, required profile fields, three story seeds, and a voice anchor sample. Used by scripts/run-onboarding.ts.

ingestion.intakeSummary: Alex Rivera, BS Computer Science (UGA, 2025, GPA 3.82), applying to MS in AI / CS-AI programs for Fall 2026 intake. Strong NLP and multi-agent RL background. Requires full tuition coverage plus stipend, so GA / TA / RA funding is a hard requirement.

sourceDocuments.label: Alex Rivera - Resume (2026 applications)
sourceDocuments.extractedText: Alex Rivera. Bachelor of Science in Computer Science, University of Georgia, May 2025. Cumulative GPA 3.82 / 4.00. Research: undergraduate researcher, UGA NLP Lab, Sep 2023 - May 2025, advised by Prof. J. Morales. Led a project applying transformer-based retrieval to low-resource code search; contributed to an ACL workshop paper on span-level error analysis. Industry: software engineering intern, Acme Robotics, Summer 2024; improved on-device speech-to-intent latency by 38% with a distilled intent classifier. Projects: multi-agent reinforcement learning coordination for household pantry restocking (Python, PettingZoo, PyTorch); transformer-based code summarizer for Python snippets (Hugging Face Transformers); on-device speech-to-intent prototype using Conformer encoder with INT8 quantization. Coursework highlights: Machine Learning, Natural Language Processing, Deep Learning, Artificial Intelligence, Algorithms, Linear Algebra, Probability.

sourceDocuments.label: University of Georgia - Undergraduate Transcript
sourceDocuments.extractedText: University of Georgia. Bachelor of Science in Computer Science, awarded May 2025. Cumulative GPA 3.82 / 4.00 across 123 credit hours. Upper-division coursework: CSCI 4380 Data Mining (A), CSCI 4360 Data Science (A), CSCI 4540 Natural Language Processing (A), CSCI 4550 Artificial Intelligence (A), CSCI 4560 Deep Learning (A-), CSCI 4470 Algorithms (A), CSCI 4730 Operating Systems (B+), MATH 3000 Linear Algebra (A), MATH 4600 Probability (A-). Honors: Dean's List, Fall 2023 through Spring 2025. Independent study CSCI 4960R in NLP research under Prof. J. Morales, two semesters, grade A.

sourceDocuments.label: Personal voice anchor - 2025 reflective essay
sourceDocuments.extractedText:
I write in plain sentences because I want the argument to be checkable. When I describe a project I try to say the problem, the constraint, and the thing I actually changed, in that order. I would rather be specific than sound impressive. If a claim cannot be grounded in something I built or measured, I cut it.

I care about systems that learn in places where data is scarce and where a wrong answer is visible. My favorite work combines careful evaluation with a clear story about what the model is doing and why. I try to treat benchmarks as diagnostic tools, not as scoreboards, and I push back when an ablation is missing.

Outside the lab I am drawn to collaborators who treat disagreements as useful signals. I am happy to be corrected quickly, and I expect the same back. The research I want to do over the next two years sits at the intersection of language models, multi-agent coordination, and on-device inference, and I want advisors who make room for focused, scope-limited experiments over broad but thin projects.

answers.prompt: What is your full legal name?
What email should we use for your applications?
What is your citizenship or residency status?
Where did you complete your undergraduate degree?

answers.answer: Bachelor of Science in Computer Science

answers.prompt: What was your cumulative GPA and scale?
What year did you (or will you) graduate?
What degree are you applying for?
answers.answer: MS in Computer Science / Artificial Intelligence track

answers.prompt: Which intake term are you targeting?
What research or application areas are you most excited about?

answers.answer: Hard requirement for full tuition coverage plus stipend via GA / TA / RA or fellowship.

answers.prompt: Why a research-adjacent MS, in one paragraph?
answers.answer: I want two focused years to go from being an enthusiastic undergraduate researcher to someone who can own a non-trivial research project end to end. My undergrad NLP work and multi-agent RL project convinced me that the combination of language-grounded reasoning and coordination problems is where I want to spend my time, and I want access to a lab that takes that combination seriously.

answers.prompt: List your most important projects or experiences.
answer: UGA NLP Lab - retrieval-augmented code search, span-level error analysis
Multi-agent RL pantry coordination with PettingZoo and PyTorch
Acme Robotics internship - on-device speech-to-intent latency reduction
Transformer code summarizer using Hugging Face Transformers

answers.prompt: In your own words, describe how you like to write about your work.
answers.answer: Plain sentences, one argument per paragraph, claims grounded in something I built or measured.

value: UGA NLP Lab - retrieval-augmented code search

story_seeds.title_hint: Retrieval-augmented code search in the UGA NLP Lab
story_seeds.narrative: As an undergraduate researcher in the UGA NLP Lab I led a retrieval-augmented code search project that asked a narrow question: does span-level supervision meaningfully improve retrieval on low-resource code repositories? I built the evaluation harness, designed an ablation over span granularity, and ran the training loop on our shared cluster. The eventual paper contribution was a span-level error analysis that clarified which retrieval failures were about lexical drift and which were about semantic reasoning. The work was not glamorous. It was a lot of careful bookkeeping and a lot of plots that looked almost identical until one of them did not.
proof_points: Led retrieval-augmented code search project end to end as an undergraduate researcher at the UGA NLP Lab.
Designed an ablation over span granularity and built the evaluation harness.
Authored a span-level error analysis that became part of a workshop paper contribution.

source_refs.label: Onboarding answer: UGA NLP Lab - retrieval-augmented code search

verification_notes: Cross-checked against resume line about UGA NLP Lab and independent study transcript entry.

story_seeds.narrative: I built a multi-agent reinforcement learning environment that modeled a shared household pantry as a coordination game between independent restocking agents. The interesting part was not the reward function; it was the failure mode. Two agents with almost identical policies converged to a state where neither restocked anything because both expected the other to do it. I added a cheap communication channel and an explicit turn-taking prior and ran a small ablation. The result was modest but honest: with communication, throughput improved; without it, coordination collapsed. It was the first project where I felt like I was doing research, not just coursework.
proof_points: Implemented a multi-agent RL environment in PettingZoo and PyTorch around a pantry-restocking coordination game.
Diagnosed a free-rider failure mode and ran an ablation comparing no-communication baselines against a cheap communication channel.
Reported that coordination collapsed without communication and throughput improved modestly with it.

source_refs.label: Resume: multi-agent RL pantry coordination project

verification_notes: Confirmed narrative against resume project line about PettingZoo / PyTorch multi-agent RL.

story_seeds.title_hint: On-device speech-to-intent latency at Acme Robotics
story_seeds.narrative: At Acme Robotics I was handed a speech-to-intent model that ran too slowly on the target device. My job was to make it faster without making it worse. I profiled the hot path, replaced the backbone with a distilled Conformer encoder, quantized it to INT8, and added a short caching window for the most common intents. The latency came down by about 38% and the intent accuracy barely moved. I remember the summer mostly as a series of small, measurable improvements rather than a single big idea, and I think that is the honest way to talk about it.
proof_points: Replaced the speech-to-intent backbone with a distilled Conformer encoder and INT8 quantization.
Added an intent-level caching window for the most common requests to avoid recomputation.
Delivered a 38% latency reduction on the target device without hurting intent accuracy.

source_refs.label: Onboarding answer: Acme Robotics - on-device speech-to-intent

verification_notes: Matched to resume line about Acme Robotics summer 2024 and speech-to-intent latency reduction.
